1.1: Three-layer neural network with input neurons x1 x2 x3, hidden layer h1 h2 h3, and output neurons o1 o2, showing fully connected weighted edges between layers
1.2: Single neuron diagram showing 3 weighted inputs (0.6, 0.3, 0.1) feeding into one neuron that produces a single output value
1.3: Neural network layer with 3 inputs (fever, cough, fatigue) connected to 3 neurons (Flu, Cold, Allergy) producing output scores, showing how a layer processes all inputs simultaneously
Detailed weight connection diagram showing 3 inputs mapped to 3 neurons with 9 individually labeled weights, demonstrating different neurons use different weights on the same inputs
1.4: Labeled neuron anatomy diagram showing inputs with weights, sum plus bias, activation function (ReLU), and output, illustrating the complete computation inside a single neuron
1.5: Line graph of study hours vs grade showing a linear relationship that a straight line can model
Curve graph of temperature vs growth showing a parabolic peak pattern that no straight line can fit
S-curve graph of drug dose vs effect showing a nonlinear response that no straight line can fit
1.6: Line graph showing ReLU-activated neuron output that is flat at zero then linearly increases, with an annotated bend point
Line graph showing a second ReLU neuron with a different bend point, demonstrating how different weights create different activation patterns
Line graph showing the combined output of two ReLU neurons creating a valley shape that no single straight line could produce
1.7: Two-layer neural network with labeled weights and biases on every edge, used for tracing a complete forward pass computation step by step
1.8: Bar chart comparing predicted value against actual value with an annotated gap, plus a causal chain showing weights leading to prediction leading to loss
1.10: Simple neuron flow diagram showing input connected by weight to a neuron with bias, producing output, used to demonstrate derivative computation
Parabolic loss curve (loss vs weight) with a marked current point, tangent line labeled slope equals derivative, and an arrow toward the minimum
1.11: Chain rule formula rendered as partial derivatives: dLoss/dweight decomposed into three colored fractions showing the three backward pass steps
1.12: Gradient descent visualization showing a U-shaped loss curve with labeled steps and curved arrows rolling downhill toward the minimum through successive weight updates
1.13: Two-layer neural network annotated with all weights, biases, intermediate outputs, target value, loss value, and backward gradient flow arrow for backpropagation tracing
1.19: Neural network layer with 3 medical inputs fully connected to 3 neurons with 9 labeled weights and output scores, demonstrating that a layer is matrix multiplication
1.23: Line graph showing training loss decreasing steadily over epochs, illustrating a model that memorizes training data
Line graph showing validation loss that initially decreases then increases, with a Best annotation at the minimum, illustrating overfitting
Six-neuron layer with dropout showing alternating active and dropped neurons, illustrating random neuron zeroing during training
Six-neuron layer at inference showing all neurons active, illustrating that dropout is turned off during prediction
Vertical transformer block diagram showing where dropout is applied: after attention and after FFN, each with p=0.1
1.24: Momentum formula: m_t equals beta_1 times m_(t-1) plus (1 minus beta_1) times g_t, showing exponential moving average of gradients
Adaptive learning rate formula: v_t equals beta_2 times v_(t-1) plus (1 minus beta_2) times g_t squared, tracking average squared gradient magnitude per parameter
Bias correction formulas: m-hat_t equals m_t divided by (1 minus beta_1 to the power t), and v-hat_t equals v_t divided by (1 minus beta_2 to the power t), correcting for zero initialization
Full Adam update rule: theta_(t+1) equals theta_t minus alpha times m-hat_t divided by (square root of v-hat_t plus epsilon), combining momentum, adaptive rates, and bias correction
AdamW update rule: theta_(t+1) equals theta_t minus alpha times (m-hat_t divided by (square root of v-hat_t plus epsilon) plus lambda times theta_t), with decoupled weight decay applied directly
1.25: Line graph showing learning rate increasing linearly from zero during warmup phase over 2000 training steps
Line graph showing learning rate with linear warmup to a peak then smooth cosine decay to zero, illustrating the complete LR schedule
1.26: Diagram showing the symmetry problem when all weights are zero: three neurons all produce identical output, the network is stuck forever
2.3: Cross-entropy loss formula rendered as mathematical notation: Loss = -log(P_correct), with annotations explaining each term
Plotted negative log probability curve showing loss vs probability with annotated points from confident-and-right to very-wrong, demonstrating exponential penalty for low confidence
2.5: Deep neural network output layer diagram showing hidden state neurons connected through an unembedding weight matrix to vocabulary output neurons, illustrating how hidden states become word predictions
5.1: Complete Transformer architecture diagram showing encoder and decoder halves with multi-head attention, add and norm, FFN layers, positional encoding, embeddings, residual skip connections, and linear plus softmax output head
7.4: Softmax formula rendered as mathematical notation showing e to the power of each score divided by sum of all e scores, converting raw attention scores to probabilities
8.3: Feed-forward network architecture diagram showing expand-then-compress structure: 512-dim input through W1 to 2048-dim hidden with GELU activation, then W2 back to 512-dim output
8.6: Vertical Transformer encoder block stack diagram showing token embedding plus positional encoding feeding through repeated blocks (each with self-attention, add and norm, FFN, add and norm) annotated with Nx bracket
9.3: Cross-attention architecture diagram showing encoder stack producing K and V projections cached and fanned out to decoder layers, where Q comes from decoder while K and V come from encoder output
9.7: Progressive encoder-decoder training flow diagram showing complete pipeline: tokenization, embedding, positional encoding, encoder self-attention with Q K V projections, FFN, decoder with causal masking and cross-attention, and output head producing predictions at every position
9.8: Progressive encoder-decoder inference flow diagram showing autoregressive generation: encoder runs once, K V output is cached, decoder runs in a loop processing one new token at a time using KV cache for previously computed keys and values
